findings.source: What Is an Attack Vector? - IBM
findings.quote: malicious insider attacks stand out as the most costly cause of data breaches among all attack vectors, at USD 4.92 million per incident
findings.assessment: At $4.92M per incident, this benchmark is well below the current $22M VaCR, suggesting the estimate may be significantly overstated relative to average breach costs.

findings.source: Insider Threat Statistics 2025: Costs, Trends & Defense - DeepStrike
findings.assessment: The $17.4M annual aggregate cost of insider threats across businesses is still below the $22M VaCR, mildly challenging the current estimate though closer in magnitude.

findings.source: Insider Threat Statistics 2025: Costs, Trends & Defense - DeepStrike
findings.assessment: A per-incident cost of $779K for credential theft is substantially lower than $22M, strongly challenging the current VaCR unless multiple simultaneous incidents or sector-specific amplifiers are assumed.

findings.source: Insider Threat Statistics 2025: Costs, Trends & Defense - DeepStrike
findings.quote: single breach initiated by a malicious insider costs an average of $4.92 million
findings.assessment: Consistent with the IBM figure, this $4.92M average per-breach cost is far below the $22M VaCR, reinforcing that the current estimate may be overstated relative to typical single-incident losses.

findings.source: Insider Threat Statistics 2025: Costs, Trends & Defense - DeepStrike
findings.quote: credential theft is the most expensive at $779,797
findings.assessment: This per-incident figure of ~$780K for the costliest insider threat sub-type is an order of magnitude below $22M, significantly challenging the current VaCR on a per-event basis.

findings.source: Insider Threat Statistics 2025: Costs, Trends & Defense - DeepStrike
findings.assessment: At $715K per malicious insider act, this benchmark is orders of magnitude below $22M, challenging the current VaCR unless the energy sector's critical infrastructure context justifies a very large uplift.

results.agent_summary: All six benchmarks—ranging from $715K to $17.4M per incident or annually—fall well below the current $22M VaCR, consistently suggesting the estimate may be overstated relative to industry norms, though energy-sector critical infrastructure exposure and potential multi-incident or cascading loss scenarios could partially justify a premium above these averages.
